skill.name: diffdock
skill.description: Guide DiffDock molecular docking workflows for protein-ligand pose prediction, confidence interpretation, batch screening, and result analysis. It supports PDB, SMILES, SDF, and sequence-based workflows, but it does not predict binding affinity.
skill.summary: Run and interpret DiffDock protein-ligand docking workflows for pose prediction and virtual screening.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many critical and high alerts, but review found they are mostly false positives from Markdown command examples, molecular terminology, amino acid sequences, and local scientific helper scripts. The remaining real risk is operational: users are guided to clone external repositories, pull Docker images, run shell commands, launch a local web UI, and write local output files. No evidence found of credential theft, prompt injection, obfuscation, command injection, or malicious data exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: External Commands and Third-Party Downloads Required
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE with legitimate intent. The skill instructs users to clone DiffDock, create Conda environments, pull and run a Docker image, run Python modules, and optionally use an online demo. These commands are normal for DiffDock but require users to trust external repositories, images, and downloaded model assets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands and URLs are explicit and semantically tied to installing and running DiffDock. The risk is operational trust and execution risk, not evidence of malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Local File Reads and Writes in Helper Scripts
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE with limited scope. The included scripts read user-supplied CSV and result directories, validate referenced file paths, and write CSV summaries or templates. This is expected scientific workflow behavior, but users should run it only on intended local paths and review output targets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem operations are directly visible and have normal CLI-controlled scope. I did not find deletion, credential harvesting, or network transmission of file contents.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Alerts Are Mostly Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for command injection. Most external command alerts point to fenced Markdown examples or parameter names that show users how to run DiffDock, GNINA, Docker, and helper scripts. They are not hidden execution paths inside the skill runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are Markdown documentation or explicit user-run commands. I did not find code that constructs shell commands from untrusted input.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Data and Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Domain False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for credential and cryptography risk. The SAM hit is an amino acid sequence containing the substring SAM, and the weak-crypto hits map to molecular docking text, filenames, or model terminology rather than cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain biological sequences or natural-language documentation. No evidence found of Windows SAM access, hashing implementation, or weak cryptographic use.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import Is Restricted to Dependency Checks
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for malicious dynamic loading. The setup checker calls __import__ only with package names from hardcoded dependency lists to report installation status. It does not import user-provided module names.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The import target comes from fixed dependency tuples in the script. No user-controlled import path or plugin loading behavior was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Facing Shell Command Workflows
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains many shell commands for installing, running, and post-processing DiffDock workflows. These are expected for this domain but should be treated as user-approved operations in a trusted environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are explicitly documented and numerous. They are legitimate examples, but command execution remains a real operational risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: CLI-Controlled Local Output Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: Helper scripts write templates and summary CSV files to user-selected paths. This is useful but can overwrite files if users choose existing output names.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write paths are controlled through CLI arguments and normal defaults. The concern is limited to local overwrite risk, not hidden persistence or exfiltration.

content.value_statement: Protein-ligand docking workflows are hard to set up, parameterize, and interpret correctly. This skill guides DiffDock runs, batch screening, confidence review, and follow-up validation.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use DiffDock for protein-ligand pose prediction.
Provides single-complex docking commands for PDB, sequence, SMILES, SDF, and MOL2 inputs.
Guides batch CSV preparation and virtual screening workflows.
Helps tune sampling, inference steps, temperature, and batch size parameters.
Interprets DiffDock confidence scores and separates pose confidence from binding affinity.
Uses helper scripts to validate batch inputs, check setup, and summarize local results.
limitations: It does not predict binding affinity, kinetics, selectivity, or ADMET properties.
It depends on a working DiffDock installation with suitable Python, PyTorch, RDKit, and optional GPU support.
It is not designed for protein-protein docking, large peptide ligands, or covalent docking.
Predicted poses need visual inspection, complementary scoring, and experimental validation.

use_cases.title: Prepare a single docking run
use_cases.description: Set up a DiffDock command for one protein and ligand, then review expected output files and confidence scores.

use_cases.title: Screen a compound library
use_cases.description: Create a batch CSV, reuse protein embeddings, run virtual screening, and identify top pose candidates.

use_cases.title: Validate docking quality
use_cases.description: Interpret confidence scores, inspect pose plausibility, and choose follow-up scoring or experimental validation methods.

prompt_templates.title: Dock one ligand
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me run DiffDock for one protein PDB file and one ligand SMILES string. Explain the command and expected outputs.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare batch input
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a DiffDock batch plan for these protein and ligand inputs. Include CSV columns, validation steps, and output organization.

prompt_templates.title: Tune difficult cases
prompt_templates.prompt: My ligand is flexible and the confidence scores are low. Recommend DiffDock parameter changes and validation checks.

prompt_templates.title: Design screening workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a high-throughput DiffDock virtual screening workflow with precomputed embeddings, result ranking, and downstream rescoring.

output_examples.input: Dock aspirin to a prepared protein structure.
output_examples.output: A concise command plan with the protein path, ligand SMILES, output directory, and notes for checking ranked SDF poses.

output_examples.input: Summarize batch docking results with confidence scores.
output_examples.output: A ranked summary of complexes, top poses, confidence classes, and recommendations for visual inspection or rescoring.

output_examples.input: Explain whether high confidence means strong binding.
output_examples.output: A clarification that confidence measures pose certainty, not affinity, with suggested GNINA, MM/GBSA, or experimental follow-up.

best_practices: Verify the DiffDock environment before long runs, especially CUDA, RDKit, and model checkpoints.
Start with a small test complex before scaling to batch docking or virtual screening.
Use confidence scores for triage, then validate poses with inspection, rescoring, and experiments.
anti_patterns: Do not treat DiffDock confidence scores as binding affinity values.
Do not run large batch jobs before validating protein preparation and ligand formatting.
Do not use this workflow for covalent docking or large protein-protein docking tasks.

faq.question: Does this skill run DiffDock by itself?
faq.answer: It provides commands, helper scripts, and interpretation guidance. A working local or containerized DiffDock installation is still required.

faq.question: Can it predict binding affinity?
faq.answer: No. DiffDock predicts binding poses and confidence scores. Use scoring functions or assays for affinity.

faq.question: What inputs does it support?
faq.answer: It documents PDB files, protein sequences, SMILES strings, SDF files, MOL2 files, and batch CSV inputs.

faq.question: Is a GPU required?
faq.answer: A GPU is strongly recommended. CPU runs can be much slower, especially for batch screening.

faq.question: Can it help with virtual screening?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains batch CSV creation, embedding reuse, confidence ranking, and top-hit extraction.

faq.question: How should results be validated?
faq.answer: Inspect top poses, check chemical plausibility, rescore with complementary tools, and validate experimentally when possible.
